pattern: Sort jobs by end time and use DP with binary search: for each job, the best profit is either skipping it or taking it and adding the best profit from non-overlapping jobs before its start.
hints: Sort jobs by end time. For each job you face a choice: take it (earning profit plus the best you can do before its start time) or skip it.
You need the maximum profit achievable up to a certain end time; binary search on the sorted end-time array finds the latest non-conflicting job in O(log n).
Build a DP array where dp[i] holds the best profit using the first i end-time-sorted jobs; dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], profit[i] + dp[j]) where j is the largest index with end[j] <= start[i].

edge_inputs.description: two jobs that share an end/start boundary — can take both
all jobs overlap; pick the single most profitable
greedy by profit alone fails; longer cheap chain beats one expensive job
high-profit late job that requires skipping several mid-range ones
two jobs with same start and end — pick the better one

stress.description: dense random jobs over a compressed time range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    jobs = []
    for _ in range(n):
        s = rng.randint(1, 10000)
        e = rng.randint(s + 1, s + 500)
        p = rng.randint(1, 10000)
        jobs.append((s, e, p))
    starts = [j[0] for j in jobs]
    ends = [j[1] for j in jobs]
    profits = [j[2] for j in jobs]
    return [starts, ends, profits]

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def jobScheduling(self, startTime, endTime, profit):
        import bisect
        jobs = sorted(zip(endTime, startTime, profit))
        n = len(jobs)
        ends = [j[0] for j in jobs]
        dp = [0] * (n + 1)
        for i, (e, s, p) in enumerate(jobs):
            j = bisect.bisect_right(ends, s) - 1
            take = dp[j + 1] + p
            dp[i + 1] = max(dp[i], take)
        return dp[n]
